title: Greetings & Polite Phrases
description: Beyond Hallo and Tschüss — the polite phrases that make you sound like you actually live here.

words.exampleTranslation: See you Monday — have a nice weekend!
words.whyItMatters: What every German colleague says to you on Friday afternoon. Reply with 'Danke, ebenso!'

words.example: Mach's gut, wir sehen uns nächste Woche!
words.exampleTranslation: Take care, we'll see each other next week!
words.whyItMatters: Casual farewell among friends — softer than 'Tschüss', warmer than 'Auf Wiedersehen'. Formal version: 'Machen Sie's gut!'

words.example: Es war schön, dich zu sehen. Bis bald!
words.exampleTranslation: It was nice to see you. See you soon!
words.whyItMatters: Family of farewells: 'bis morgen', 'bis später', 'bis nächste Woche'. All start with 'bis' (until).

words.example: Es tut mir leid, ich kann nicht kommen.
words.whyItMatters: Genuine apology — different from 'Entschuldigung' (quick 'excuse me'). Literally 'it does me sorrow'.

words.example: Kannst du mir helfen? — Kein Problem!
words.exampleTranslation: Can you help me? — No problem!
words.whyItMatters: The default friendly reply when someone thanks you or apologizes. Synonyms: 'Kein Ding', 'Gerne'.
Standard greeting at events, in stores, at airports. 'Herzlich' (heartfelt) makes it warmer than plain 'willkommen'.

words.example: Du gehst ins Kino? Viel Spaß!
words.exampleTranslation: You're going to the cinema? Have fun!
words.whyItMatters: Daily-use phrase. 'Spaß' (fun) is masculine — 'der Spaß'. Other versions: 'Viel Glück' (good luck), 'Viel Erfolg' (success).

words.example: Du fliegst nach Indien? Gute Reise!
words.exampleTranslation: You're flying to India? Have a good trip!
words.whyItMatters: Said to anyone traveling. 'Die Reise' (the trip) is feminine. Cousin phrase: 'Gute Fahrt!' (good drive).

words.exampleTranslation: All the best for your birthday!
words.whyItMatters: Universal good-wish phrase — birthdays, new jobs, farewells. Add 'zum + occasion': 'Alles Gute zum neuen Job!'
What you say when someone sneezes. Literally means 'health'. The noun 'die Gesundheit' = health (feminine).
Standard reply to 'Danke'. Also said when handing something over ('here you go'). 'Bitte sehr' is interchangeable.

words.example: Das Essen ist fertig. Guten Appetit!
words.exampleTranslation: The food is ready. Enjoy your meal!
words.whyItMatters: Said before every meal in Germany — almost mandatory at restaurants and family tables. Reply: 'Danke, dir auch / Ihnen auch!'

title: Family & People
description: Beyond mum and dad — the extended family and social circles you actually talk about.

words.example: Meine Verwandten kommen aus dem Süden.
words.exampleTranslation: My relatives come from the south.
words.whyItMatters: Plural-only when meaning 'family members in general'. Singular 'der/die Verwandte' = a specific male/female relative, declines like an adjective.
Pronounced French-style: 'koo-ZEE-neh'. Male version: 'der Cousin' (also French pronunciation: 'koo-ZÄNG').

words.exampleTranslation: My uncle works as a teacher.
words.whyItMatters: Plural 'die Onkel' identical to singular. Female counterpart: 'die Tante'. No 'great-uncle' word — say 'der Großonkel'.
Pattern: 'Schwieger-' + family member = '-in-law'. So 'der Schwiegervater', 'der Schwiegersohn', 'die Schwiegertochter' all work the same way.
Weak masculine noun — accusative 'den Nachbarn', dative 'dem Nachbarn'. Female form: 'die Nachbarin'. Plural 'die Nachbarn'.

words.example: Er ist nicht mein Freund, nur ein Bekannter.
words.exampleTranslation: He's not my friend, just an acquaintance.
words.whyItMatters: Critical social distinction in German: 'der Freund' is reserved for close friends; 'der Bekannte' covers most people you know but aren't close with. Declines like an adjective.

words.example: Die Oma freut sich über ihren Enkel.
words.exampleTranslation: Grandma is happy about her grandson.
words.whyItMatters: Female: 'die Enkelin'. Plural 'die Enkel' (grandchildren) is identical to singular. Often paired with 'die Großeltern'.

words.example: Wir besuchen unsere Großeltern am Sonntag.
words.exampleTranslation: We're visiting our grandparents on Sunday.
words.whyItMatters: Plural-only — no singular form 'der Großelter'. For one grandparent, say 'der Opa' / 'die Oma' (or formally 'der Großvater' / 'die Großmutter').
Watch: 'ein Paar' (capital P) = a couple/pair (matching); 'ein paar' (lowercase) = a few/some. Different meanings!
Weak masculine noun: accusative/dative 'den/dem Kollegen'. Female form 'die Kollegin'. Plural 'die Kollegen'.
Pronounced French-style: 'SHEF'. Female: 'die Chefin'. In Germany you usually address your boss with 'Sie' even if friendly.

words.example: Die Leute hier sind sehr nett.
words.exampleTranslation: The people here are very nice.
words.whyItMatters: Plural-only. More colloquial than 'die Menschen'. Use it for 'people' in everyday speech: 'viele Leute', 'die Leute denken...'

title: Food & Meals
description: Order, taste, complain, compliment — the words that turn a survival eater into a real diner.

words.example: Eine warme Mahlzeit am Tag ist wichtig.
words.exampleTranslation: One hot meal a day is important.
words.whyItMatters: 'Mahlzeit!' alone is also a German workplace greeting around lunchtime — equivalent to 'enjoy lunch' or just 'hi'.

words.exampleTranslation: For breakfast I eat bread rolls.
words.whyItMatters: Verb form: 'frühstücken' (to have breakfast). 'Ich frühstücke um sieben Uhr.' Other meals: 'das Mittagessen', 'das Abendessen'.

words.exampleTranslation: For dessert there is ice cream.
words.whyItMatters: Also called 'der Nachtisch' (more common in spoken language). 'Zum Nachtisch' is the typical phrase at restaurants.
Used for restaurants AND online shopping. 'Was möchten Sie bestellen?' is what a waiter will ask you.

words.example: Das Essen schmeckt mir sehr gut.
words.exampleTranslation: The food tastes very good to me.
words.whyItMatters: Takes DATIVE: 'mir schmeckt es', not 'mich'. Works like 'gefallen'. 'Schmeckt's?' = 'Tasting good?' (waiter question).

words.example: Das Curry ist zu scharf für mich.
words.exampleTranslation: The curry is too spicy for me.
words.whyItMatters: Also means 'sharp' (knife) or 'in focus' (image). Comparative 'schärfer', superlative 'am schärfsten' — both with umlaut.

words.example: Mama, das Essen ist sehr lecker!
words.exampleTranslation: Mom, the food is very tasty!
words.whyItMatters: Everyday word — much more natural than 'köstlich' (sounds formal/old-fashioned). Native speakers use 'lecker' constantly.

words.example: Nein danke, ich bin schon satt.
words.whyItMatters: Essential after-meal word. Idiomatic 'jemanden satt haben' = to be fed up with someone.

words.example: Können Sie mir die Speisekarte bringen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you bring me the menu?
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'die Speise' (dish) + 'die Karte' (card). For drinks specifically: 'die Getränkekarte'.
Universal restaurant phrase — say it confidently to get the check. 'Zahlen, bitte!' works too. Tip is usually 5-10%.
More formal than 'was zu trinken'. Plural 'die Getränke' is common on menus and in supermarket sections.

words.exampleTranslation: Would you like to try the cheese?
words.whyItMatters: Used for food/drink/clothing — anything you sample before buying. Don't say 'versuchen' for tasting food.

title: Days, Time & Dates
description: Schedules, calendars, frequency — talk about when things happen with precision.

words.whyItMatters: Specifically a public/religious holiday — NOT vacation. For vacation use 'der Urlaub'. Plural 'die Feiertage'.
Latin-origin noun, neuter. Plural is irregular: 'die Daten' (which also means 'data' in tech contexts — same word!).

words.example: Warte eine Sekunde, ich komme gleich!
words.whyItMatters: Used like in English — 'a moment'. For real precision: 'in einer Sekunde'. Plural 'die Sekunden'.

words.exampleTranslation: In the 21st century we live digitally.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'das Jahr' + 'hundert'. Said as 'einundzwanzigsten Jahrhundert' for the 21st. Plural 'die Jahrhunderte'.

words.exampleTranslation: Please come on time to the appointment.
words.whyItMatters: Cultural note: Germans take being 'rechtzeitig' very seriously. Slightly different from 'pünktlich' (exactly on time).
Frequency spectrum: nie → selten → manchmal → oft → immer. Adjective 'selten' = rare ('eine seltene Sprache').

words.exampleTranslation: We should see each other more often.
words.whyItMatters: Comparative of 'oft' (often). Note the umlaut. 'Öfter mal was Neues' (try something new more often) is a common saying.
'Einen Moment' is the universal 'just a sec' — say it in shops, on the phone, anywhere you need a brief pause. Plural 'die Momente'.
Two meanings: (1) very soon, (2) equal/same. Context tells you which. 'Gleich' for immediate future is more spoken than 'sofort'.
Beyond 'already' it's also a flavoring particle: 'Das ist schon gut' (that's OK / good enough). Position usually after the verb.
'Schon' and 'noch' are mirror words. 'Schon fertig' (already done), 'noch nicht fertig' (not yet done), 'noch ein Bier' (one more beer).

words.exampleTranslation: Sometimes I work on the weekend.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first: 'Manchmal arbeite ich...' (verb in position 2). Middle of frequency scale: between 'selten' and 'oft'.

title: Everyday Verbs
description: Twelve verbs that go beyond sein/haben — the engines of real A1 conversation.

words.whyItMatters: Takes accusative. With infinitive use 'zu': 'Du brauchst nicht zu kommen' (you don't need to come). Regular conjugation.
Used for durations: 'Wie lange dauert das?' (how long does this take?). Don't use 'nehmen' for time durations — that's English interference.
Takes DATIVE: 'gehört mir/dir/ihm', not 'mich/dich/ihn'. Don't say 'gehören zu' for possession — just dative directly.
Takes 'sein' as auxiliary in Perfekt: 'ist passiert', not 'hat passiert'. Like all '-ieren' verbs, no 'ge-' in Partizip II.
Two meanings: (1) to shine (sun, light), (2) to seem ('Es scheint, dass...'). Context tells you which.

words.example: Ich glaube, dass er recht hat.
words.whyItMatters: 'Ich glaube' is the everyday 'I think/suppose' — softer than 'denken'. Takes dative for people: 'Ich glaube dir' (I believe you).
Vowel-change verb: ich vergesse, du vergisst, er vergisst. Partizip II 'vergessen' (no 'ge-' because of the 've-' prefix).
Strong verb: Partizip II is 'verloren'. Also used for losing a game/match: 'Wir haben das Spiel verloren'.
No preposition needed — takes accusative directly. Don't say 'suchen für' or 'suchen nach' for objects you've lost.

words.exampleTranslation: I think this film is boring.
words.whyItMatters: Two meanings: (1) to find (a thing), (2) to think/find (opinion). 'Ich finde X gut' is the A1 way to give opinions.
Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'Ich bin geblieben'. Strong verb: bleiben → blieb → geblieben.

words.example: Kannst du mir den Weg zeigen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you show me the way?
words.whyItMatters: Takes both dative (person) AND accusative (thing): 'Ich zeige dir das Foto'. Word order: dative person before accusative thing.

title: Useful Adjectives
description: Twelve adjectives that let you describe people, situations, and things with actual personality.

words.whyItMatters: Often paired with people: 'ein freundlicher Mensch'. Regular comparative: freundlicher, am freundlichsten.
Classic German virtue word — culturally loaded as positive. Opposite: 'faul' (lazy). Common in school contexts.
Literally 'new-greedy' — wanting to know new things. Often pairs with 'auf + Akkusativ': 'neugierig auf das Ergebnis'.
Noun 'die Geduld' (patience). Opposite 'ungeduldig'. Common phrase: 'Geduld haben' (to have patience).
'Ehrlich gesagt' (honestly speaking) is a daily filler phrase. Noun 'die Ehrlichkeit' = honesty.

words.exampleTranslation: She's a bit shy at first.
words.whyItMatters: Pronunciation tip: 'SHÜKH-tern'. Often softened with 'etwas' (somewhat) or 'ein bisschen' (a little).

words.exampleTranslation: Our living room is very cozy.
words.whyItMatters: Iconic German word with no exact English match. Describes warmth, comfort, low pressure. Also for people: 'eine gemütliche Person' (relaxed).
Often pairs with 'Tag', 'Arbeit', 'Reise'. Verb 'sich anstrengen' (to make an effort). Different from 'müde' (tired — describes the person).
Use for funny things AND fun situations. Different from 'witzig' (witty, clever-funny). 'Spaßig' is a softer alternative.
Describes a thing, NOT a person feeling bored. For 'I am bored', use 'Mir ist langweilig' (dative) or 'Ich langweile mich' (reflexive).
Comparative 'wichtiger', superlative 'am wichtigsten'. Common pairings: 'wichtiger Termin', 'wichtige Entscheidung', 'wichtige Person'.
Synonym 'schwer' is also common (and means 'heavy' too). 'Schwierig' specifically means challenging/complex — slightly more formal.

title: Trennbare Verben
description: Twelve essential separable verbs — the most common splittable verbs in everyday German.

words.example: Ich stehe jeden Morgen um sieben auf.
words.exampleTranslation: I get up at seven every morning.
words.whyItMatters: Splits in present: 'ich stehe um sieben auf'. Perfekt: 'bin aufgestanden' (uses sein!). Strong verb.
Splits in present: 'er ruft mich an'. Takes ACCUSATIVE (the person you call). Perfekt: 'hat angerufen'.
Splits: 'ich kaufe Brot ein'. 'Einkaufen gehen' is the most common phrase (compound infinitive). Perfekt: 'hat eingekauft'.

words.exampleTranslation: Are you coming along to the cinema?
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'kommst du mit?'. Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'ist mitgekommen'. The yes/no question form is super common.

words.example: Der Zug kommt um drei Uhr an.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'der Zug kommt an'. Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'ist angekommen'. 'In + Dativ' for the destination.

words.exampleTranslation: We get off at the station.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'wir steigen aus'. Opposite: 'einsteigen' (get in/on). Takes 'sein': 'bin ausgestiegen'.

words.exampleTranslation: I like watching TV in the evenings.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich sehe fern'. Vowel change: 'du siehst, er sieht'. Perfekt: 'hat ferngesehen'. The noun is 'der Fernseher' (TV).

words.example: Der Film fängt um acht an.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'der Film fängt an'. Vowel change: 'du fängst, er fängt'. Synonym 'beginnen' (NOT separable). Perfekt: 'hat angefangen'.

words.example: Ich hole dich um acht ab.
words.exampleTranslation: I'll pick you up at eight.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich hole dich ab'. Used for people, packages, or things from a place. Perfekt: 'hat abgeholt'.

words.example: Was hast du am Wochenende vor?
words.exampleTranslation: What are you planning this weekend?
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich habe etwas vor'. Standard question for asking about plans. Perfekt: 'hat vorgehabt'.
Splits: 'ich mache das Fenster auf'. More informal than 'öffnen'. Perfekt: 'hat aufgemacht'.
Splits: 'er macht die Tür zu'. Informal alternative to 'schließen'. Perfekt: 'hat zugemacht'. Pair with 'aufmachen' for doors/windows.
